title: The Aged Stranger
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I was with Grantן¿½ the stranger said;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said the farmer, ן¿½Say no more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But rest thee here at my cottage porch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For thy feet are weary and sore.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I was with Grantן¿½ the stranger said;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said the farmer, ן¿½Nay, no more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I prithee sit at my frugal board,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And eat of my humble store.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How fares my boy, my soldier boy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the old Ninth Army Corps?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I warrant he bore him gallantly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the smoke and the battleן¿½s roar!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I know him not,ן¿½ said the aged man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, as I remarked before,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was with Grantן¿½ ן¿½Nay, nay, I know,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said the farmer, ן¿½say no more:

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He fell in battle, I see, alas!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thouן¿½dst smooth these tidings oן¿½er,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nay, speak the truth, whatever it be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though it rend my bosomן¿½s core.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How fell he?ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his face to the foe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upholding the flag he bore?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh, say not that my boy disgraced
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The uniform that he wore!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I cannot tell,ן¿½ said the aged man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And should have remarked before.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I was with Grant, in Illinois,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some three years before the war.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the farmer spake him never a word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But beat with his fist full sore
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That aged man who had worked for Grant
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some three years before the war.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
